Nepal's flood early warning system for Imja glacial lake in the Everest region has been abandoned and deteriorating since 2016. The equipment designed to detect dangerous water level rises has not been maintained for years.

**Immediate danger:** Thousands of people living downstream—primarily in the Khumbu valley—have no advance warning if the glacial lake suddenly drains or overflows. A catastrophic release could send a wall of water down the valley with no alert system to evacuate.

**Why this matters:** Glacial lakes are inherently unstable. As glaciers melt from climate change, they create larger lakes behind unstable ice dams. These can fail suddenly. Without monitoring equipment, residents have only minutes to hours of natural warning signs (if any) instead of potentially days.

**Who's affected:** Local communities, trekking routes, and infrastructure including the Sherpa settlements that depend on tourism and agriculture in this region.

**Root cause:** Lack of funding and maintenance responsibility. Once installed, no organization took ongoing ownership of the system, so it was simply left to deteriorate.

This is a straightforward institutional failure—functional infrastructure was allowed to fail through neglect, leaving vulnerable populations unprotected from a known natural hazard.
